responses.response: The offset argument is empirically well-founded per the scenario's own documentation and it directly reinforces rather than undermines my opposition to S2 — I already cited this exact mechanism as the core reason employers lose out (fragmentation of the state-controlled identification channel). Where I'd push back on the coalition framing is the implicit assumption that this makes S2 uniformly bad for all stakeholders equally: for employers specifically, the harm is not just 'equity gain minimal' but a concrete operational cost — loss of visibility into where talent is being trained. A reform that produced the same offset but paired it with mandatory reporting/transparency requirements on non-state selective intake would still have the offset, but would neutralize the employer-specific harm (opacity) even if the equity harm remained. So the fact base is shared but the decision-relevant harm for my interest is narrower and more addressable than the general equity framing suggests.

This argument sharpens rather than weakens my case: if the reform's own operational burden (sustained medium-to-high administrative cost, decade-long dual regime) is conceded even by proponents, that confirms the instability cost I already flagged as harming employer planning. I'd add one nuance the teacher/union framing doesn't fully capture: for employers, the workload/burden problem matters less in itself than as a leading indicator of reversal risk — a reform that visibly strains its own implementers is a reform more likely to be politically abandoned, which is precisely the risk that makes multi-year recruitment and curriculum-alignment planning unviable. This doesn't change my opposition; it reinforces the same condition I already set (cross-party pact demonstrated to survive a government change) as necessary before any support.

I acknowledge the cross-national evidence on delayed tracking is stronger than I gave it credit for in isolation — Hanushek & Woessmann is not merely 'weak/model-knowledge,' it is a well-evidenced international pattern. But my objection to S3 was never that the cross-national mechanism is fictional; it's that the Hungary-specific transmission channel (peer effects) is conceded even by proponents as weak, and that the transferability gap is exactly where the flight/offset risk (A12) becomes decisive. Strong cross-national evidence for a different institutional context does not resolve whether the mechanism survives contact with Hungary's non-state sector and choice architecture. So this argument updates my confidence that delayed tracking *can* work somewhere, without changing my assessment that it is unproven to work *here* under current safeguards.

This value claim from conservative and church-maintainer voices converges with part of my S4 assessment (institutional continuity, preserved selective pathways) but I don't fully endorse its optimism that S4 'raises the floor without lowering the ceiling' as an achieved fact rather than an aspiration. The same evidence gap I cited against S4 (no comparator has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age) applies here too — A17 asserts compatibility of excellence and equity that the scenario's own evidence section does not support. My conditional stance already reflects this: S4 preserves the pipeline (which A17 values), but I do not share A17's confidence that this simultaneously solves the equity problem non-disruptively. Where A17 sees resolved compatibility, I see an untested claim requiring the pilot validation I already specified.
